questions.questionText: The antidote for opioid overdose with respiratory depression is:
questions.explanation: Naloxone is a competitive opioid receptor antagonist reversing respiratory depression.

questions.questionText: The antidote for acetaminophen toxicity is:
questions.explanation: N-acetylcysteine replenishes glutathione and reduces hepatic injury in acetaminophen poisoning.

questions.questionText: A cholinergic toxidrome with salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, and miosis suggests poisoning by:
questions.explanation: Organophosphate exposure causes acetylcholinesterase inhibition and cholinergic excess.

questions.questionText: Initial antidotal therapy in organophosphate poisoning includes:
questions.explanation: Atropine counters muscarinic effects and pralidoxime reactivates acetylcholinesterase when given early.

questions.questionText: A high anion gap metabolic acidosis with visual symptoms is classically seen in:
questions.explanation: Methanol metabolizes to formic acid causing severe acidosis and optic toxicity.

questions.questionText: Preferred antidotal therapy for methanol poisoning includes:
questions.explanation: Fomepizole inhibits alcohol dehydrogenase and prevents toxic metabolite formation.

questions.questionText: A patient exposed to smoke inhalation with headache and cherry-red skin should be suspected to have:
questions.explanation: CO poisoning causes tissue hypoxia from carboxyhemoglobin formation; pulse oximetry may be misleading.

questions.questionText: The definitive treatment in severe carbon monoxide poisoning is:
questions.optionB: 100 percent oxygen and consider hyperbaric oxygen in severe cases
questions.explanation: High-flow oxygen reduces carboxyhemoglobin half-life; hyperbaric oxygen is used for selected severe cases.

questions.questionText: The classic acid-base disorder in salicylate toxicity is:
questions.optionB: Mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: Salicylate poisoning causes early respiratory alkalosis with later high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

questions.questionText: A key urinary alkalinization indication in toxicology is:
questions.explanation: Urinary alkalinization enhances renal salicylate excretion in appropriate patients.

questions.questionText: The antidote for methemoglobinemia in symptomatic patients is:
questions.explanation: Methylene blue reduces methemoglobin back to hemoglobin in most cases.

questions.questionText: In unknown poisoning with reduced consciousness, the immediate priority in emergency management is:
questions.explanation: Initial toxicology management always prioritizes airway, breathing, and circulation.

questions.questionText: Iron poisoning in children presents with which classic stages?
questions.optionB: Stage 1: GI (vomiting, bloody diarrhea, 0-6h) → Stage 2: latent improvement (6-24h) → Stage 3: systemic toxicity (metabolic acidosis, shock, hepatic necrosis, 12-48h)
questions.explanation: Iron poisoning stages: (1) GI phase (0-6h): nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, hematemesis. (2) Latent/quiescent phase (6-24h): apparent improvement (misleading). (3) Systemic toxicity (12-48h): metabolic acidosis (anion gap), shock, hepatic necrosis, coagulopathy, renal failure. (4) Late (2-8 weeks): GI scarring/strictures. Serum iron >500 μg/dL: severe toxicity. Antidote: deferoxamine (iron chelator — urine turns vin rosé/red-brown). Abdominal X-ray may show iron tablets. Reference: Harrison — Iron Poisoning.

questions.questionText: Lead poisoning in children classically presents with:
questions.optionB: Abdominal colic, constipation, lead lines on gums (Burton lines), basophilic stippling on blood smear, wrist/foot drop (adults), encephalopathy (children)
questions.explanation: Lead poisoning: GI (colicky abdominal pain, constipation), neurologic (encephalopathy in children — most dangerous, peripheral neuropathy/wrist drop in adults), hematologic (microcytic anemia, basophilic stippling, ringed sideroblasts, inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase), renal (Fanconi-like), Burton lines (blue-black line on gingival margin). Diagnosis: blood lead level (>5 μg/dL: elevated). Treatment: chelation — EDTA (CaNa2EDTA), succimer (DMSA — oral, children), dimercaprol (BAL). Reference: Harrison — Lead Poisoning.

questions.questionText: Flumazenil is the specific antidote for overdose of:
questions.optionB: Benzodiazepines (competitive antagonist at GABA-A benzodiazepine site — caution: may precipitate seizures)
questions.explanation: Flumazenil: competitive antagonist at benzodiazepine binding site on GABA-A receptor. Reverses sedation and respiratory depression from benzodiazepine overdose. Caution: (1) May precipitate seizures in chronic benzodiazepine users, (2) May unmask co-ingested proconvulsant drugs, (3) Short half-life (risk of re-sedation). Not used routinely in undifferentiated overdose. Benzodiazepines alone rarely fatal in overdose (unlike combined with opioids/alcohol). Naloxone: opioid antidote. NAC: acetaminophen. Reference: Harrison — Flumazenil.
